lines.text: APPENDIX — Quick reference
Key numbers

Estimated global phage count: ~10^31 Phages in your gut: ~10^12 Phages per milliliter of seawater: ~10^7 T4 genome size: ~170 kb, ~300 genes Lambda genome size: ~48 kb, ~70 genes M13 genome size: ~6.4 kb, 11 genes (single-stranded DNA) Phi-X174 genome: ~5.4 kb, 11 genes — first DNA genome ever sequenced (Sanger, 1977) Phage particle size: ~50-200 nm
Key dates

1915: Twort observes "glassy patches" in bacterial cultures 1917: d'Hérelle independently discovers phages in dysentery patients; coins "bacteriophage" 1923: Eliava Institute founded in Tbilisi 1937: Eliava executed under Stalin 1940: Phage group forms around Delbrück and Luria at Cold Spring Harbor 1943: Luria-Delbrück fluctuation experiment 1952: Hershey-Chase experiment establishes DNA as genetic material 1977: Sanger sequences phi-X174 — first complete DNA genome sequence 1985: George Smith

publishes phage display in Science 2005: Mojica realizes CRISPR spacers are phage-derived 2006: FDA approves ListShield phage spray for Listeria on deli meats 2017: Tom Patterson saved by emergency phage therapy at UCSD 2018: Nobel Prize in Chemistry to Smith and Winter for phage display 2019: Spencer cystic fibrosis case at Great Ormond Street uses engineered phage
Key model phages

T4: lytic, E. coli, classic lunar lander morphology, large genome, the phage group's workhorse Lambda (λ): lysogenic/lytic switch, E. coli, used for cloning vectors and for the CI-Cro switch story M13: chronic, non-lytic, filamentous, E. coli (F+), the phage display chassis P22: Salmonella, transduction tool, lysogenic T7: lytic, E. coli, fast lifecycle, used as expression vector and vaccine display platform Phi-X174: lytic, E. coli, tiny genome, first sequenced
Key concepts

Lytic: phage kills host immediately; analogy: wolf Lysogenic: phage integrates as prophage, can switch to lytic later; analogy: sleeper agent Plaque assay: one phage particle, one visible plaque in a bacterial lawn — gold-standard count method Host range: which bacterial strains a phage can infect, determined by surface receptor specificity CI / Cro switch: lambda's bistable lysogeny/lytic switch, the foundational model for cell-fate decisions Cocktail: multiple phages combined for broader
coverage and resistance prevention Phage-antibiotic synergy: combined therapy can outperform either alone Endotoxin removal: a major manufacturing challenge for systemic phage products
Key clinical cases to remember

Tom Patterson, UCSD, 2016-17: multi-drug-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii, rescued by emergency phage therapy, became the rallying example for the resurgence; wife Steffanie Strathdee's book The Perfect Predator Spencer, Great Ormond Street, 2019: cystic fibrosis patient, Mycobacterium abscessus, engineered lytic phage from Graham Hatfull's Pittsburgh lab
Key companies (as of 2026)

Intralytix — first major Western phage company, ListShield food safety product Adaptive Phage Therapeutics — therapeutic phage cocktails Locus Biosciences — CRISPR-armed phages Armata Pharmaceuticals — therapeutic phage Pherecydes Pharma (France) — therapeutic phage, several compassionate-use successes Felix Biotechnology — phage-antibiotic synergy BiomX (Israel) — phage products for inflammatory bowel disease, atopic dermatitis
Key institutions

Eliava Institute, Tbilisi, Georgia — the historical center of phage therapy, continuous operation since 1923 Hirszfeld Institute, Wrocław, Poland — Eastern European phage therapy center IPATH, UCSD — Center for Innovative Phage Applications and Therapeutics, founded 2018, first major U.
S. academic phage therapy center Cold Spring Harbor — historical home of the phage group
One-line takeaways

More phages on Earth than stars in the visible universe Phages are precision predators, not broad-spectrum poisons The original molecular biology was done on T-even phages and lambda Phage therapy never died in the East; the West forgot it and is now relearning CRISPR is bacterial anti-phage immunity — phages gave us the most important biotech tool of the century ListShield is in your supermarket — phages are already commercial The frontier is in-pharmacy phage matching: sequence the bug, match
the library, dispense in 48 hours
